commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: The remaining blocker is maintainer security-boundary and owner approval, not a narrow automated repair.; Cleared: No concrete security flaw was found in the inspected diff; the remaining security item is maintainer acceptance of the intentionally expanded, gated Browser capability surface. (sha=e523468acab0815ab2fc6b19fb7ad5c02b18eac8)
commands.review_summary:
The PR expands the Browser plugin Chrome MCP existing-session surface with additional debug, interaction, artifact, extension, emulation, cleanup, config, docs, and test coverage.

PR surface: Source +4335, Tests +4378, Docs +60, Other +23. Total +8796 across 48 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. this is primarily a feature/security-boundary expansion rather than a current-main bug report. The PR body supplies after-fix live-output proof for the branch behavior.
commands.review_followup: The remaining blocker is maintainer security-boundary and owner approval, not a narrow automated repair.

target.title: feat(browser): expand Chrome MCP web capabilities
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session transport only exposed a small subset of the browser automation and debug surface that was available in the local testbed, so real browser workflows still fell back to partial behavior.
- Solution: route the Chrome MCP transport through the richer Browser tool surface, including console/network diagnostics, text and coordinate interactions, dialogs, emulation, tracing, heap snapshots, Lighthouse, screencasts, extension inventory, and safer existing-session cleanup.
- What changed: updates are scoped to Browser plugin runtime/tests under `extensions/browser/...`; focused coverage was added for Chrome MCP routing, existing-session behavior, wrapper forwarding, live-surface behavior, CDP helpers, and profile cleanup ownership.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not modify live OpenClaw config, Gateway service config, credentials, Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant configuration, personal Chrome profiles, or `CHANGELOG.md`.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the Browser plugin changes in this PR and ran the real behavior proof and local validation described below.

## Motivation

- This makes the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session mode useful for full web workflows instead of only basic navigation/click/readback.
- It also tightens cleanup behavior so dedicated OpenClaw-managed profiles can be stopped safely without targeting arbitrary personal or custom Chrome profiles.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Chrome MCP-backed Browser sessions now expose and execute the broader live web capability surface from a real OpenClaw setup, while preserving dedicated-profile cleanup boundaries.

Real environment tested: Linux host, Node/pnpm source checkout, OpenClaw Browser plugin with a dedicated `agent-chrome` profile, Chrome MCP existing-session transport, local loopback test fixture, and redacted/non-public runtime details.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
git diff --check
timeout 300s corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.live.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.helpers.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.screenshot-params.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/config.test.ts
timeout 240s corepack pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/browser/tsconfig.json --noEmit
corepack pnpm check:changed
```

Evidence after fix: copied live output/readback from the same host after validation:

```text
git diff --check: pass
focused Browser vitest: 7 passed (7); 196 passed | 1 skipped (197)
Browser TypeScript check: pass
check:changed: pass

live suite: 55 pass/evidence lines plus 3 expected guardrails
status/profile/listing: pass
tabs/navigation/snapshot/click/hover/fill/select/key/resize/dialog/upload: pass
fetch + networkidle: pass
drag/drop/evaluate/console/network inspection: pass
screenshot/emulation/trace/heap snapshot/Lighthouse/screencast: pass
extension list/install/reload/tab-id/uninstall: pass
third-party and WebMCP discovery: pass
tab close/browser stop/cleanup: pass

post-validation readback:
Gateway active/running; NRestarts=0
agent-chrome status: enabled=true, driver=existing-session, transport=chrome-mcp, running=false, pid=null
host-owned agent-chrome/chrome-devtools-mcp/watchdog leftovers: none
```

Observed result after fix: the Browser plugin exercised the expanded Chrome MCP web capabilities against a real local setup, generated non-empty runtime artifacts for screenshot/trace/heap/Lighthouse/screencast flows, cleaned up the dedicated Browser profile, and left the Gateway running without a restart.

What was not tested: public CI had not run at PR creation time; cross-OS behavior was not tested locally; a pipe-backed existing-session PDF path still needs reachable HTTP CDP and was treated as an expected guardrail; local `web_fetch` of private loopback URLs remained blocked by SSRF/private-network policy as expected; no Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant or credential behavior was exercised.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the pre-patch Chrome MCP path only covered a narrower Browser surface; the after-fix proof above demonstrates the expanded surface end to end.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Chrome MCP support existed, but Browser tool routing and existing-session handling had not been expanded to the full tested browser capability surface.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no focused coverage proving the Browser tool roster and Chrome MCP wrappers could execute the richer live surface.
- Contributing context (if known): several flows need CDP or Chrome MCP-specific routing instead of generic Browser fallbacks.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.live.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.existing-session.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.helpers.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.screenshot-params.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/config.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Chrome MCP Browser routing, existing-session startup/readback, cleanup ownership, CDP helper behavior, and live-capability wrapper forwarding.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the focused Browser tests exercise the changed plugin surface without running unrelated package suites.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A for the expanded surface; existing Browser tests covered narrower behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; focused tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Browser sessions using Chrome MCP existing-session transport can use more Browser tool capabilities for real web workflows.
- Dedicated Browser profile cleanup is stricter about ownership and should avoid stopping arbitrary personal/custom Chrome profiles.
- Unsupported/guarded cases remain explicit instead of silently pretending to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Browser tool request] -> [Chrome MCP existing-session path] -> [partial surface / fallback gaps]

After:
[Browser tool request] -> [Chrome MCP existing-session path] -> [routed Browser capability] -> [real browser result + focused proof]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the Browser plugin exposes additional browser automation/debug capabilities when the Browser tool is enabled. Mitigations in this PR include dedicated-profile cleanup ownership checks, explicit unsupported-state handling, focused tests around existing-session ownership, and preserving the existing Browser/Gateway permission model. The live proof used a dedicated Browser profile and redacted local runtime details.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin, Chrome MCP existing-session transport
- Relevant config (redacted): dedicated `agent-chrome` Browser profile, no personal profile or credential mutation

### Steps

1. Run the focused Browser validation commands listed in Real behavior proof.
2. Start a dedicated Browser profile using the Chrome MCP existing-session transport.
3. Exercise tabs, navigation, interactions, diagnostics, artifacts, extension inventory, and cleanup against a local loopback fixture.
4. Read back Browser status and host process cleanup.

### Expected

- Chrome MCP-backed Browser sessions execute the expanded web capability surface.
- Unsupported guardrails stay explicit.
- Dedicated-profile cleanup leaves no owned Chrome MCP/process leftovers.
- Gateway remains running.

### Actual

- Focused tests, Browser TypeScript check, `git diff --check`, and `check:changed` passed.
- The live host suite recorded 55 pass/evidence lines and 3 expected guardrails.
- Post-validation readback showed Gateway active/running with `NRestarts=0`, Browser profile stopped, and no host-owned leftovers.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Evidence is summarized in copied output above. Local live artifacts included screenshot, Chrome trace, heap snapshot, Lighthouse JSON/HTML, and a non-empty WebM screencast; they are not committed to the repository.

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused Browser tests, Browser TypeScript check, changed-file gate, live Browser capability gauntlet, Browser status readback, and cleanup readback.
- Edge cases checked: unsupported PDF path without reachable HTTP CDP, SSRF/private-network guardrail for local `web_fetch`, unavailable third-party/WebMCP executable page tools, dialogs, upload, drag/drop, extension inventory, and cleanup ownership.
- What I did **not** verify: cross-OS behavior, public CI before PR creation, Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant behavior, credential/auth behavior, and local `codex review --base origin/main` because the standalone Codex CLI is intentionally unavailable on this host.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No bot review conversations existed at PR creation time.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: expanded Browser capability surface may expose more powerful browser automation when Browser is enabled.
  - Mitigation: preserve existing Browser permission/config boundaries and add focused tests for routed capabilities.
- Risk: cleanup code could accidentally target the wrong Chrome profile.
  - Mitigation: cleanup is gated around OpenClaw-managed dedicated profile ownership and covered by focused tests plus live readback.
- Risk: Chrome MCP/CDP capabilities can differ by environment.
  - Mitigation: unsupported states remain explicit, and the PR includes live proof plus focused unit/seam coverage.
target.author: acskamloops

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
